test_id_1.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Please note: The practitioner is expected to limit the NYS Medicaid claim amount to the actual invoice cost
of the drug dosage administered and maintain that documentation.
Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for
additional program policy and billing guidance. It is recommended that providers use electronic claims
submission whenever possible. These changes should ease practitioner burden by decreasing paper claim
submission and associated invoice documentation for claims with a DOS on or after October 1, 2024.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
5.0 Utilization Management Programs
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the current provider manual says Medicare or Part D is primary and Medicaid is secondary. The manual also says the combined payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, while the policy is broader and mainly reinforces Medicare-first billing.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, no additional Medicaid payment is made. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed before Medicaid. This policy is broader than the manual because it does not state the pharmacy-specific payment cap. It still supports Medicare-first coordination for dual eligible claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicare members who also have Medicaid must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plan formulary, or the plan exception and appeals process, for needed prescriptions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill Medicare before Medicaid for dual eligible members because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. This policy does not address Part D enrollment or formulary rules. It is therefore less specific than the manual on pharmacy coverage requirements.

similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be used before Medicaid for dual eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available reimbursement sources for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual adds a pharmacy-specific payment limit and Part D coverage rules, while the policy update stays at a general payer-of-last-resort level [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
answers.caveats: The policy source is older and more general, so it does not match the manual’s pharmacy-specific Part D guidance as closely.

answers.headline_summary: For dual eligible pharmacy claims, the current provider manual says Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and any payment must be accepted as payment in full. The policy evidence also says Medicaid is the payer of last resort and Medicare must be billed first, but it is broader and older than the current manual guidance.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligible pharmacy claims, total Medicare plus Medicaid payment cannot exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. Any Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed before Medicaid for dual enrollees. This is a broader billing-sequence rule and does not state the current payment cap. It is less specific than the provider manual on how the final payment is limited.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies may also need Medicare enrollment depending on their business model. The manual ties this to current Medicare enrollment expectations for pharmacy applicants.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update does not give the dual eligible Medicare enrollment rule for pharmacies. It instead directs providers to pharmacy billing guidance and claim-entry instructions, including ordering and prescribing National Provider Identifier fields in restricted-member situations. That makes it a partial match only.

similarities: Both sources address how Medicare and Medicaid interact for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

Both sources treat Medicare as part of the billing sequence before Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
differences: The provider manual gives the current payment cap and payment-in-full rule, while the policy update only states the broader payer-of-last-resort rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The provider manual also addresses Medicare enrollment and assignment expectations for pharmacies, but the policy update does not cover that topic and instead points to billing and claim-field instructions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The policy source is older and broader than the provider manual, so the manual should be treated as the more current operational guidance.

answers.headline_summary: The current provider manual says dual-eligible pharmacy claims are generally driven by Medicare first. Medicare Part D must be used for covered drugs, and Medicare and Medicaid payment together cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount. The older Medicaid Update reinforces the payer-of-last-resort rule, saying other sources such as Medicare must be billed before Medicaid, but it is broader and less pharmacy-specific than the manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual-eligible members, Medicare Part D applies first for covered drugs. Medicaid does not cover Part D-covered drugs for full-benefit dual eligibles. Any Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare and other reimbursement sources must be billed before Medicaid. The policy states the general billing order for dual enrollees, but it is not as pharmacy-specific as the manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members. This applies regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. The manual also says pharmacies should determine whether Medicare enrollment is needed before Medicaid pharmacy enrollment.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must bill Medicare and other third-party sources before billing Medicaid because Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement sources are exhausted. The policy gives the general coordination-of-benefits rule, but it does not spell out the pharmacy enrollment steps.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If a pharmacy carries items that must be billed to Medicare for a dual-eligible member, enrollment in the relevant Medicare type is required. The manual ties the requirement to the pharmacys actual billing needs. It gives a concrete enrollment trigger for pharmacies that handle Medicare-billed items.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicare must be billed before Medicaid for dual enrollees. The policy supports the same billing sequence, but it is broader and does not state the pharmacy enrollment trigger.

similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be used before Medicaid for dual-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available reimbursement sources [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual is more specific to pharmacy billing and enrollment, while the Medicaid Update states a broader dual-eligible billing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The provider manual adds the Part D coverage rule and the payment-in-full limit, but the Medicaid Update does not address those pharmacy-specific details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
answers.caveats: The policy source is older than the provider manual, so the manual should be treated as the current operational guidance for this comparison.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.

orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a

Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is generally the payer of last resort. Other liable coverage must be billed first, and Medicaid only pays the remaining patient responsibility or allowed amount when the claim is correctly submitted.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual eligibles, total payment to the pharmacy cannot exceed the amount that would have been paid for a Medicaid-only patient. If Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment is made. Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be recovered from the dual eligible member, and full-benefit dual eligibles must be enrolled in Medicare Part D for covered Part D drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid requires pharmacies to coordinate with the proper primary payer before billing Medicaid. The policy is broader than the manual because it focuses on billing order and coordination of benefits rather than Medicare-specific payment limits. It also says pharmacies must submit under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, when other coverage exists.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacy provider must pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. If another party is responsible as primary payor, that party must pay first, and Medicaid only considers payment after other resources are used. Claims with other coverage may deny at point of service, and correctly submitted claims can pay the patient responsibility or the Medicaid allowed amount in limited cases.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid says providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, and Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. The policy matches the manual on the basic order of payment. It is less specific because it does not spell out the manuals point-of-service denial language or the detailed claim-processing exceptions.

similarities: Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available coverage and limit Medicaid payment to the remaining patient responsibility or allowed amount when the claim is correctly submitted [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds Medicare-specific limits, including that combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount and that Part B deductible and coinsurance cannot be collected from the dual eligible member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader and focuses on coordination of benefits and the proper primary payer, including the correct coverage benefit pathway, rather than Medicare payment caps [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.

available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).

with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The pharmacy must bill any responsible third party first, and Medicaid pays only after other coverage has been exhausted.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider manual says all available resources must be used before Medicaid pays. If another party is the primary payor, that party must pay first, and Medicaid then pays patient responsibility or the Medicaid-allowed amount after coordination of benefits. Claims with other coverage may be denied at point of service with response code 717.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The Medicaid Update says providers must bill liable third parties before NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer before billing Medicaid. The policy also says Medicaid pays patient responsibility for correctly submitted coverable claims up to the Medicaid allowed amount.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligibles, the manual says they must be enrolled in Medicare Part D. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Part D for those members. Members must use the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process for needed prescriptions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The Medicaid Update is broader and does not specifically mention Medicare Part D. It says pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer and submit claims under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, before billing Medicaid.

similarities: Both sources say other coverage must be billed before Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary or last resort coverage for these claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific about Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligibles, while the Medicaid Update stays at the broader coordination of benefits level [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual is more explicit and current on dual-eligible co-payment responsibility. For Medicare Part B benefits, the pharmacy cannot seek to recover Medicare deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, and the payment must be accepted as payment in full.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part B benefits, a pharmacy provider cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. The manual also states that total Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so Medicare must be billed first for dual enrollees. If Medicare covers the encounter, Medicaid may reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible only to the extent permitted by New York State law. The policy is broader and does not state the no-collection rule as directly.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part D dual-eligible members, Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs covered under Part D. Medicaid only pays for certain excluded drug classes, and claims submitted to Medicaid remain subject to Medicaid rules. This guidance is specific to the pharmacy benefit context.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For dual enrollees, potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare must be billed before Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The policy states the billing order, but it does not give the same Part D drug coverage detail. It is therefore less specific than the manual on pharmacy claims.

similarities: Both sources require Medicare to be billed before Medicaid for dual-eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:
Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other coverage for dual-eligible claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:
differences: The provider manual directly says the pharmacy cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals, while the policy only says Medicaid may reimburse Part B cost sharing to the extent permitted by New York State law. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:

The provider manual gives a pharmacy-specific rule for Part D and excluded drugs, while the policy gives only the general last-resort billing rule. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:
answers.caveats: The policy source is older and less specific on co-payment responsibility, so the provider manual should be treated as the more current operational guidance.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider-manual guidance says Medicare and Medicaid payments for dual-eligible members must be accepted as payment in full, and pharmacies cannot seek Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from those members. The policy evidence is less direct on co-payment responsibility itself; it mainly reinforces that duals are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is payer of last resort, with Medicaid only paying certain Medicare-related cost-sharing when permitted by law.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part B services, the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full. The pharmacy cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals. This is a direct billing prohibition for the pharmacy provider.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so it pays only after other reimbursement sources are exhausted. For dually enrolled Medicare and Medicaid members, Medicaid may reimburse Medicare Part B coinsurance and deductible only to the extent permitted by New York State law. This is broader policy guidance rather than a pharmacy billing prohibition.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part D members, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug except in the limited situations listed in the manual. Claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization. The manual also says Medicaid does not cover drugs that are covered under Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The Medicaid Update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It also emphasizes that Medicare flexibilities and Medicare requirements apply first. The policy is broader than pharmacy billing and does not state a specific co-payment collection rule.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare as the primary payer framework for dual-eligible members. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7, New York State Medicaid Program:

Both sources limit Medicaid payment for duals to narrow circumstances rather than treating Medicaid as the first source of payment. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1, New York State Medicaid Program:
differences: The provider manual gives a direct pharmacy rule that Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full and that Part B deductible or coinsurance cannot be collected from dual-eligible individuals. The policy update is less specific and only says Medicaid may reimburse Part B cost-sharing to the extent allowed by New York State law. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two:

The provider manual also addresses Part D billing limits and says Medicaid is not appropriate for most Part D drugs. The policy update instead speaks at a higher level about duals being subject to Medicare rules and does not address pharmacy co-payment collection directly. [New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The policy source is broader than the provider manual and does not give an equally direct co-payment collection rule for pharmacies.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Refusing to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided with services or goods, sending bills or any other
legal means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, and dual-eligible members cannot be charged Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. The policy evidence is narrower and only directly confirms the same no-billing rule for Medicare Part C dual-eligible members, so it supports the broader no-cost-sharing theme only partially.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals, the pharmacy provider cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. The manual gives a direct no-billing rule for this Medicare cost-sharing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For Medicare Part C dual-eligible individuals, the provider cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance directly from the member. The provider must accept Medicare Advantage and Medicaid payment as payment in full. The policy statement is narrower because it is limited to Part.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment. The manual states that providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of ability to pay. This is a direct Medicaid copayment protection.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The retrieved policy update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not directly state Medicaid copayment collection rules or inability-to-pay protections. The policy side is therefore only an indirect match for this topic.

similarities: Both sources say the member cannot be billed for the relevant cost-sharing when the payment rules apply [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources require the provider to accept the applicable Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual directly addresses Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the policy update only directly addresses Medicare Part C co-payment and co-insurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual also gives a direct rule that providers may not refuse service when a Medicaid member cannot afford a copayment, but the policy update does not directly address that issue [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy update is broader and less specific than the provider manual on ordinary Medicaid copayment responsibility for dual-eligible members.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual gives the clearest current pharmacy rule: Medicare and Medicaid payments for dual-eligible members must be accepted as payment in full, and pharmacies may not recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from those members. The policy update is related but broader, because it says duals are subject to Medicare rules without spelling out the pharmacy copayment collection rule in the same detail.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicare Part B services, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment as payment in full. The pharmacy may not seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare and Medicaid dual-eligible individuals. This is a direct pharmacy billing rule.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The July 2025 Medicaid Update says duals are subject to Medicare rules. It does not itself spell out a pharmacy rule for collecting Medicare cost-sharing from dual-eligible members. The policy statement is broader and less specific than the manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For dual-eligible members, the manual says Medicaid is not appropriate to bill for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless an exception applies. It also says claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization. The manual is focused on Medicaid billing rules for dual members rather than member copayment collection.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The May 2024 Medicaid Update says a Medicare Part C provider cannot attempt to recover any copayment or coinsurance amount directly from dual-eligible individuals. It requires the provider to accept the plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full. It also says the member may not be billed for any Part C copayment or coinsurance not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both sources treat dual-eligible billing as limited by Medicare-related rules. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7, New York State Medicaid Program:
differences: The provider manual gives a direct pharmacy-specific prohibition on recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts, while the July 2025 policy update only says duals are subject to Medicare rules and does not state that billing prohibition in the same detail. [New York State Medicaid Program:

The policy update includes a separate Part C example that bars billing the member for unreimbursed copay or coinsurance, while the provider manual row is centered on pharmacy billing and Medicaid rules for dual-eligible claims. [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5, New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The two sources address different Medicare pathways, so the policy update is only a partial match to the pharmacy manual guidance on co-payment responsibility.

Pharmacies may use the following override to remove the copayment for these scenarios:
In the National Council for Prescription Drugs Program field 461-EU, enter a value "04" (Exempt Copay and/or
Coinsurance). Providers may also refer to the following resources:
• New York State Department of Health (NYS DOH) Office of Health Insurance Programs (OHIP)
Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication
ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy

.pdf.

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.

• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug is specifically excluded from Part D by law and is on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. The policy update is narrower and older, but it supports the same core rule for dual eligibles.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid should not be billed for a Medicare Part D member’s prescription drug unless the drug is one Medicaid may pay for because it is specifically excluded from Part D by law. The manual gives examples such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. Claims for dual-eligible members are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D. This update is narrower because it focuses on the listed copayment override scenarios rather than the full coverage rule. It does not add a broader exception for Part D-excluded drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligibles, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and their prescribers should work together to find a drug covered by the Part D plan or use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals process for needed prescriptions not on the formulary.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: This update is only indirectly related because it explains a copayment override and points readers to billing resources. It does not describe the Part D exception and appeals process. It is therefore less specific than the manual on what to do when Part D does not cover the drug.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid is not the payer for drugs that Medicare Part D covers for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].

Both sources keep the focus on Medicare Part D coordination rather than treating Medicaid as the primary drug benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual gives the current coverage rule for Part D-excluded drugs and names examples, while the policy update is narrower and mainly addresses a copayment override for specific scenarios [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].

The provider manual tells members and prescribers to use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals process when needed, but the policy update does not give that workflow and instead points to billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].
answers.caveats: The policy update is older and only partially matches the row topic, so the provider manual should be treated as the controlling current guidance.
