December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of

dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

provider_manual.text:
Additional information regarding the Medicare Part D Prescription Drug Program and residential health care
facilities may be accessed at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/.
See Section 4.0 for Long Term Care Pharmacy Short Cycle Billing information.
Items Provided by Child (Foster) Care Agencies
Please see this link for items provided by Childcare Agencies:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/carveout.htm.
OMH Residential Treatment Facility Prescription Drug Carve-Out
Reimbursement of prescription drugs for children and youth between the ages of five and twenty-one who are
residents of the Office of Mental Health (OMH) Residential Treatment Facilities (RTF) is a NYRx benefit and billed
directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy.
Physician administered drugs, OTC drugs, medical supplies, immunization services (vaccines and their administration),
nutritional supplies, sick room supplies, adult diapers, and durable medical equipment (DME) are not carved out of the
RTF rate and remain the responsibility of the facility.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. The policy update is more workflow-oriented and says providers should still pursue the correct primary payer path before billing Medicaid.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid should not be billed for a Medicare Part D members prescription drug unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid reimbursable list that are Part D-excluded, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If a claim is rejected by the primary payer, coverage should still be pursued through the correct primary benefit path. The update is broader than the manual because it addresses primary-payer routing, including Medicare Part D and Medicare Part B billing issues.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers should seek a Part D-covered alternative or use the Part D plans exception and appeals process for needed prescriptions not on the formulary.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: When a primary payer rejects a claim, providers should still pursue coverage, including obtaining prior authorization from the primary payer. The update also points to the correct Medicare Part D or Part B billing path, but it does not specifically discuss Part D formulary exceptions.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare Part D as the first coverage path before Medicaid is used [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require the provider to resolve coverage through the appropriate primary payer process before expecting Medicaid payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual gives the substantive rule for Part D-excluded drugs and names examples such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader and workflow-focused. It says rejected claims should still be pursued through the correct primary benefit path, including Part D versus Part B billing and other third-party issues [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.

Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members unless the drug is specifically excluded from Part D by law and is on the Medicaid reimbursable list. The policy evidence is consistent on the core rule that dual-eligible members should use Medicare Part D first, but it is narrower and more operational, focusing on copayment override handling rather than the full coverage rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid should not be billed for a Medicare Part D members prescription drug unless the drug is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage. Medicaid only pays for drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All dual-eligible claims remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy does not restate the coverage rule for Part D-excluded drugs. It gives a billing override for exempt copayment or coinsurance situations instead. In field 461-EU, pharmacies may enter value "04" for that purpose.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Full-benefit dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Part D for those members. Members and prescribers should use the Part D formulary, exception, and appeals process first.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy is narrower and does not describe the full coordination-of-benefits rule. It only addresses a claims override for exempt copayment or coinsurance situations. That makes it a billing instruction rather than a coverage standard.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare Part D as the primary pathway for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
differences: The provider manual states the actual coverage exception for drugs excluded from Part D by law, while the policy update only gives a copayment or coinsurance override and does not explain the coverage rule. [New York State Medicaid Program:
answers.caveats: The policy source is only a partial match because it addresses an NCPDP billing override, not the underlying Part D exclusion rule.

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.

policy.text:

• New York State Department of Health (NYS DOH) Office of Health Insurance Programs (OHIP)
Standard Companion Guide Transaction Information, located at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/
5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf;
• eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication
ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy
/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20(D.0)
.pdf.
Questions:
• Billing questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343 9000.
• Questions regarding this guidance should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit at
‑
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

provider_manual.text:
Drugs will be evaluated on a case-by-case basis to determine if they are appropriate to add to the
pharmacy formulary.
Certain practitioner-administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and may be
billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that practitioner-administered drugs
must be dispensed as a Pharmacy benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician’s Manual found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a pharmacy benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to the
site of administration. This is considered “white bagging” and is acceptable under the following guidelines:
• Drugs should only be dispensed by the pharmacy directly to the patient when they are to be self-
administered. The policy surrounding self-administered drug delivery is found in section titled Delivery
above.

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says Medicaid generally does not pay for drugs covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members, except for drugs that are specifically excluded from Part D by law and appear on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. The Medicaid Update is consistent with the exception-based approach, but it is narrower because it mainly gives an exempt copayment and coinsurance override rather than restating the full coverage rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid should not be billed for a prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage and is on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. Select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs are examples of that exception. Claims for dual-eligible members are still subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The Medicaid Update gives a billing override for exempt copayment and coinsurance scenarios. It does not directly restate the Part D exclusion rule, so it is broader on claim processing and less specific on coverage. The guidance points pharmacies to use field 461-EU with value 04 for those exempt scenarios.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. For drugs covered by Part D, members and prescribers must use the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The Medicaid Update only addresses how to remove copayment or coinsurance in certain exempt situations. It does not describe the members Part D enrollment or the exception and appeals process, so it is a partial match to the workflow. The policy is consistent with a special-processing step rather than ordinary Medicaid payment.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare Part D as the primary payer framework for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].

Both sources recognize that Medicaid involvement is limited to special exception handling rather than routine payment for Part D-covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual states the actual coverage rule for Part D-excluded drugs, while the Medicaid Update mainly gives a copayment and coinsurance override for exempt scenarios [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].

The provider manual names the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and examples such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, but the Medicaid Update does not restate those coverage details [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2025 Volume 41 Number 2].
answers.caveats: The policy update is a partial match because the retrieved chunk focuses on exempt copayment and coinsurance processing, not the full Part D exclusion rule.

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid when a claim is covered by both programs. The policy update says the same general rule because Medicaid is the payer of last resort and other coverage must be billed first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For pharmacy claims covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, Medicare benefits must be maximized first. The pharmacy must bill Medicare before Medicaid. Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must submit claims to other coverage first. The policy is broader because it applies to third-party coverage generally, not only Medicare.

similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before NYS Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it says Medicare must be billed first for claims covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, while the policy update states a broader third-party billing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual also says Medicaid can be billed only after Medicare payment information is received, but the policy update does not add that Medicare-specific timing detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Current provider manual guidance says pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid for dual-eligible pharmacy claims. The policy update says the same general coordination of benefits rule applies, but it is broader and less specific than the provider manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For pharmacy claims covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, Medicare benefits must be maximized first. The pharmacy must bill Medicare before Medicaid and may bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. The manual also says the pharmacy may not bypass Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare-covered member.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and all other available resources must be used first. The policy also tells pharmacies to submit claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.

similarities: Both sources require another payer to be billed before Medicaid is billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it names Medicare and says Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy update states the broader third-party billing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

answers.headline_summary: Current NYS Medicaid pharmacy guidance says Medicaid is the payer of last resort, so a pharmacy must bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs. The provider manual is more explicit that Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must bill Medicare first. Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received. The manual also says the pharmacy must bill the appropriate Medicare benefit and use the Medicare-required code format.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing Medicaid. Pharmacies must submit claims to the proper primary payer first because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The policy is broader and does not spell out the Medicare billing sequence as specifically as the manual.

similarities: Both sources require the pharmacy to bill the other available payer before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources treat Medicaid as the payer of last resort for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it says Medicare must be billed first for services covered by both programs and that Medicaid can be billed only after Medicare payment information is received [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it says providers must exhaust other coverage and bill the proper primary payer first, but it does not give the same Medicare-specific billing instructions [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.

seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.

plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny

(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual says pharmacies must bill Medicare first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, and Medicaid can be billed only after Medicare payment information is received. The policy update says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and pharmacies must submit claims to the proper primary payer first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must bill Medicare first. Medicaid can be billed only after payment information is received from Medicare. The manual also says the pharmacy must use the Medicare billing procedure that the Medicare plan requires.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must submit claims to the proper primary payer first. The policy is broader because it applies to all liable third parties, not only Medicare.

similarities: Both sources require another payer to be billed before Medicaid when that payer is responsible for the claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources place Medicaid in a secondary position after the primary payer has been used [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is Medicare-specific for pharmacy claims, while the policy update is broader coordination of benefits guidance for all liable third parties [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual adds that Medicaid billing may occur only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy update does not give that Medicare-specific timing detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must bill Medicare first. similarities: Both sources require other coverage to be billed before Medicaid when another payer is available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
12

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will require prior authorization (PA) for most medically necessary therapy visits,
unless the member receiving the service meets one or more of the criteria listed below:
• Children from birth to age 21 (until their 21st birthday)
• Members with developmental disabilities (members with restriction/exception code "95" on file)
• Members with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) [members with TBI* (use procedure code modifier ST on
claims) or restriction/exception code "81" on file]
• Members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid (dually eligible) when the service is covered by
Medicare
• Members receiving rehabilitation services as a hospital inpatient
• Members receiving rehabilitation services in a nursing home in which they reside
• Members receiving rehabilitation services provided by a certified home health agency (CHHA)
*TBI as defined in Public Health Law Article 27-cc: §2741.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

relaxed (e.g., claims will not deny) during the transition period which starts April 1, 2023.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare has already approved and paid for the service or procedure. The older policy material also confirms that Medicare crossover claims are treated differently from the separate EOB attachment requirement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. This applies even when Medicaid would otherwise have required prior approval. The rule is stated as a specific crossover-claim exception.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update does not address prior approval directly. It says Medicare crossover claims are not subject to the EOB attachment requirement that applies to some other Medicaid claims. That makes it a related but broader billing exception.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid requires other available coverage to pay first before Medicaid considers payment. If another payer is responsible, that payer must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not override the primary payors responsibility.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting to Medicaid. It also says Medicaid is the payor of last resort. Medicare crossover claims are excluded from the EOB attachment requirement.

similarities: Both sources treat Medicare crossover claims as a special coordination-of-benefits situation under Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

Both sources support the idea that Medicare or another primary payer must be handled before Medicaid pays [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].
differences: The provider manual gives the direct prior approval rule for crossover claims, while the policy update is broader and focuses on the EOB attachment requirement instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

The policy update is older and does not state the prior approval exception as directly as the provider manual does [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Policy and Billing
Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. The November
2021 issue of the Medicaid Update article titled Reminder: NYS Requires Coordination of Benefits, located
at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/no13_2021-11.htm#cob, directed
providers to exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the Medicaid program. If a Medicaid member has third-
party insurance coverage, the benefits of that coverage must be fully used before billing the NYS Medicaid
program. Providers should always ask a Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure
the proper coordination of benefits.
All claims submitted for members with Medicare and/or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect
payments and denials received from other insurers to allow correct calculation of Medicaid reimbursement
amounts.

answers.headline_summary: For crossover claims, Medicaid prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. The older policy materials also say that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and that other coverage must be billed first.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service or procedure. This applies even when the service or procedure would otherwise have required Medicaid prior approval. The manual states this as the current rule for crossover claims.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing Medicaid, because Medicaid is the payor of last resort. Claims with Medicare or other third-party insurance must accurately reflect payments and denials from those insurers. The policy is broader and does not state the crossover prior-approval exception.

similarities: Both sources address claims that involve Medicare and Medicaid coordination of benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
Both sources require the claim to reflect the other payers involvement correctly before Medicaid payment is determined [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual gives the specific crossover exception for prior approval after Medicare payment, while the policy update only gives the broader third-party billing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

answers.headline_summary: For crossover claims, Medicaid prior approval is generally not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure, even when Medicaid would otherwise require prior approval. Other coverage must still be billed first, and Medicaid remains the payer of last resort.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Providers must pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. If a responsible third party should pay first, that party must pay first, and claims with other coverage deny at point of service unless they are correctly submitted through coordination of benefits.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting to Medicaid. Medicare crossover claims are excluded from the explanation of benefits attachment requirement, which is broader than the pharmacy manual’s point-of-service denial rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. This applies even when the service would otherwise have required Medicaid prior approval.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
12

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) will require prior authorization (PA) for most medically necessary therapy visits,
unless the member receiving the service meets one or more of the criteria listed below:
• Children from birth to age 21 (until their 21st birthday)
• Members with developmental disabilities (members with restriction/exception code "95" on file)
• Members with a traumatic brain injury (TBI) [members with TBI* (use procedure code modifier ST on
claims) or restriction/exception code "81" on file]
• Members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid (dually eligible) when the service is covered by
Medicare
• Members receiving rehabilitation services as a hospital inpatient
• Members receiving rehabilitation services in a nursing home in which they reside
• Members receiving rehabilitation services provided by a certified home health agency (CHHA)
*TBI as defined in Public Health Law Article 27-cc: §2741.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization Programs
The Medicaid program requires prior authorization for certain drugs under the following programs:
• Preferred Drug Program (PDP)
• Brand When Less Than Generic Program (BLTG)
• Dose Optimization Initiative
• Clinical Drug Review Program (CDRP)
• Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Program
• Mandatory Generic Drug Program (MGDP)
Prescribers may need to obtain prior authorizations for certain drugs. Only prescribers or their authorized agent may
submit a request for a prior authorization. Third party requests are prohibited: prescribers may not contract with or
assign authority to dispensing pharmacists, manufacturers, or other persons or companies to initiate their PA requests.
General information on the prescription drug prior authorizations, including the above programs, can be found at the
following website: https://newyork.fhsc.com.
Note: If a prior authorization number has not been obtained by the prescriber and the pharmacist is unable to reach
the prescriber, the pharmacist may obtain a prior authorization for up to a 72-hour emergency supply for any drug
that requires prior authorization, subject to state laws and Medicaid restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For crossover claims, Medicaid prior approval is generally not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure, even when Medicaid would otherwise require prior approval. If Medicare did not approve or pay, the claim must still meet the normal Medicaid prior approval rules for that service.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Providers must bill other available coverage before Medicaid. The responsible third party must pay first, and claims with other coverage are denied at point of service if they are not correctly submitted.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Providers must first bill Medicare or other third-party insurance before submitting a claim to Medicaid. The policy is broader than the provider manual because it also discusses an Explanation of Benefits attachment requirement, while excluding pharmacy claims and Medicare crossover claims from that requirement.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. This applies even when Medicaid would otherwise require prior approval.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Medicaid fee-for-service requires prior authorization for most medically necessary therapy visits unless the member meets listed exceptions. One listed exception is dual eligible members when the service is covered by Medicare, which is narrower and less specific than the provider manual crossover rule.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid is secondary when Medicare or other coverage is available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15].

Both sources recognize a Medicare-related exception to the usual Medicaid prior approval or prior authorization rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual gives the direct crossover rule for pharmacy claims, while the policy update is broader and discusses therapy visits rather than pharmacy crossover claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5].

The policy update lists a dual eligible exception for services covered by Medicare, but it does not state the same explicit crossover prior approval rule found in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
