answers.headline_summary: For crossover claims, Medicaid prior approval is generally not required after Medicare has approved and paid the service. The provider manual also says that other coverage must pay first, and prior authorization does not override the primary payor rule.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If another insurer is the primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first before Medicaid considers payment. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the primary payors responsibility. Claims with other coverage may be denied at point of service if they are not correctly coordinated.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy says any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party before the claim is submitted to Medicaid. It is broader than the provider manual because it is not limited to crossover claims. It also says failure to submit to the third party results in denial.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, prior approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for the service or procedure. This applies even when Medicaid would otherwise require prior approval. The manual states this rule directly for crossover claims.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy says Medicaid fee-for-service prior authorization is waived for members with both Medicare Part B and Medicaid when the service is covered by Medicare. That is a close match, but it is stated more broadly than the crossover-claim rule in the provider manual. It does not use the same crossover wording.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid does not require prior approval when Medicare has already covered the service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5].

Both sources also treat other coverage as a prerequisite that must be handled before Medicaid payment is considered [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual is specific to Medicaid and Medicare crossover claims, while the policy update is broader and describes a dual-eligible prior authorization exception for Medicare-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2021 Volume 37 Number 5].

The provider manual also states that failure to obtain prior authorization does not override the primary payors responsibility, which the policy update does not say in the same crossover-specific way [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

test_id_31.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.

test_id_32.query_content: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
COS 0441 Non bordering state:
Medicaid will accept applications for consideration for limited pharmacy enrollment for pharmacies in a non bordering
state location, when criteria under “Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies” below is met at time of application submission and
when there is an unmet need such as:

• The pharmacy serves a NY Medicaid member that lives in the pharmacy’s service location (e.g., a NY
Medicaid enrolled foster care child, or a NY Medicaid member residing in a NY Medicaid enrolled
residential healthcare facility as defined by Article 28) or
• The pharmacy has an exclusive arrangement to dispense a limited distribution drug on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs found here: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, that is
unavailable from an enrolled pharmacy
Medicaid Enrolled Pharmacies (COS 0441, 0161, 0288)
• Follow all federal and NYS laws, regulations, and Medicaid policies.
• Are fully operational, open, and dispensing medications.
• Are fully enrolled in Medicare* as a participating provider.
• Accept Medicare claims assignment and submit Medicare claims for all Medicare covered services on behalf
of a Medicaid member.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;

• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Fee-for-Service Dental Claim Submission Guidance
When Third-Party Liability Payment Involved
New York State (NYS) Medicaid continues to work to increase provider compliance with properly reporting
correct Coordination of Benefits (COB) information on claims submitted for beneficiaries who have primary
insurance. The COB claim to NYS Medicaid must accurately reflect the primary payers’ adjudication of the
claim as reported in the Explanation of Benefits (EOB).
When the primary insurance is a Medicare Advantage Plan / Medicare Part C with dental coverage:
• Claims for services involving a third-party payment by a Medicare Advantage Plan greater than zero
dollars (>$0) must be submitted using the electronic claim format. This also includes electronic
submission of by-report procedure codes if a payment was made by a Medicare Pact C Plan.
• The payor code for Medicare Advantage Plans is the 16-health maintenance organization (HMO) and
must be indicated on all electronic claims when Medicare Advantage is primary.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
To qualify for Medicaid payment, a compounded prescription must include:
• A combination of any two (2) or more legend drugs found on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription
Drugs, or
• A combination of any legend drug(s) included on the list of Medicaid Reimbursable Prescription Drugs and any
other item(s) not commercially available as an ethical or proprietary product(s) or
• A combination of two (2) or more products which are labeled “Caution: For Manufacturing Purposes Only”.
For example:
• The combination of Aquaphor and Hydrocortisone Cream 2.5% is NOT considered a compound since it does
not meet any of the above requirements.
• Intravenous prescription products that require reconstitution, further measurement, dilution and/or instillation
into a suitable device (i.e., mini-bag, IV reservoir or syringe) for administration are not considered to have been
compounded and should not be submitted for reimbursement as compounds.
NYS Medicaid compound policy:
• Only the dispensing pharmacy may prepare the prescribed compounded prescription.
• Dispensing outsourced prepared compounds is not allowable.
• Compounds trademarked by pharmacies are not coverable.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: For COB, NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Providers must bill any applicable third-party coverage first and then bill Medicaid for the remaining patient responsibility on correctly submitted coverable claims, up to the Medicaid allowed amount.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the claim if applicable. If the member has an open Workers’ Compensation case for a work-related injury, Workers’ Compensation must be used before billing NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid because Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must exhaust existing benefits and coordinate with the proper primary payer first. The policy is broader than the manual because it does not single out the Workers’ Compensation exception.

similarities: Both sources say Medicaid is the payer of last resort and that other liable coverage must be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require providers to coordinate with the proper primary payer before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual adds a specific pharmacy rule for an open Workers’ Compensation case and says Workers’ Compensation must be used first, while the policy update stays broader and does not name that exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2019 Volume 35 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible for preparing and delivering the drug in accordance with administration
guidelines in the package insert, as well as the replacement of improperly stored, lost, or stolen drugs until
confirmed receipt by the authorized agent.
• The pharmacy must confirm an appropriate administration plan is in place for home deliveries.
• The pharmacy is required to obtain documentation of delivery by the receipt of a signature of a recipient or
an authorized agent at the site of administration.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs that were not deliverable must be reversed within 14 days.
• Once delivered and signed for, the site of administration is responsible for replacement of improperly
stored, handled, lost, or stolen practitioner-administered drugs.
“Brown bagging” is when drugs designated for self-administration or practitioner administration are dispensed directly
to a patient by the pharmacy. Brown bagging practitioner administered drugs causes concern regarding proper
storage or handling, which can affect the drug’s efficacy. Brown bagging is acceptable only when the drug is intended,
prescribed, or labeled for self-administration.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist does not have to enter the member’s address, age, or CIN if that information is already available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. The policy evidence is narrower and does not directly address a general missing-information rule for prescriber details.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter those items on the prescription when the information is already in the pharmacy’s records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist also is not required to enter them when the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy says the pharmacy must use professional judgment when filling prescriptions in an emergency department context if the assigned primary care provider is unavailable. It also says the pharmacy must contact the assigned primary care provider the next business day. This is a narrower rule than the provider manual’s missing patient-information exception.

similarities: Both sources recognize that pharmacists may need to rely on judgment when key information is not immediately available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific exception for missing member address, age, or CIN, while the policy update is limited to emergency department prescriptions and primary care provider verification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
answers.caveats: The provided policy evidence does not directly state a general duty for missing prescriber information, so the comparison is limited to the patient-information exception and the related emergency verification context.

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies are not required to generate and keep a hard copy of electronic prescriptions and fiscal orders if they are
securely stored and maintained. When stored electronically, the electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders
that were e-prescribed must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be
authenticated.
Telephone Orders
Prescribers may telephone prescriptions and fiscal orders for drugs directly to a pharmacy unless otherwise prohibited
by state or federal law or regulations. The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the
prescriber's identity and validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
• Telephoned non-controlled prescription drug orders are considered original.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist may not need to enter the missing address, age, or CIN if that information is already available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. For missing prescriber information, the pharmacist must use professional judgment and make a good-faith effort to verify the prescriber and the prescription, especially for telephone orders when the prescriber is unknown.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid members address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if the information is already readily available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist also does not have to enter it if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy is broader and less specific on this point. It says the pharmacy must use professional judgment in an emergency-related restricted-recipient situation when the assigned PCP is unavailable, and it must contact the PCP the next business day. It does not specifically address missing patient identifiers.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: For telephone orders, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescribers identity and the validity of the prescription if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. This is a direct responsibility tied to telephone orders and unknown prescribers.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy is only a partial match and is broader. It requires professional judgment for emergency prescriptions and follow-up to the assigned PCP the next business day. It does not specifically state the unknown-prescriber verification duty.

similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy to use judgment when key information is not immediately available [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources allow action to continue with follow-up when the situation cannot be fully verified at the time of filling [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing patient identifiers and unknown prescribers, while the policy update is broader and focuses on emergency restricted-recipient workflow and PCP follow-up [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual says the pharmacist is not required to enter missing address, age, or CIN in defined circumstances, but the policy update does not address those identifiers at all [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist may not need to enter the address, age, or CIN if that information is already readily available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. When required prescriber information is missing or the prescriber is unknown, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it if the information is already readily available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill. The pharmacist also does not have to enter it if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. Controlled substance prescriptions remain subject to Article 33 and Part 80 limits on what information may be added or changed.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy does not give a direct rule for missing patient identifiers. It instead says the pharmacy must use professional judgment in emergency prescription situations and contact the assigned PCP the next business day. That is related, but it is broader and less specific than the provider manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity. This is a direct pharmacist responsibility before the prescription is treated as verified. The manual states the duty broadly and does not limit it to a special emergency setting.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy gives a narrower emergency workflow rather than a general verification rule. It says the pharmacy must use professional judgment for emergency department prescriptions when the assigned PCP is unavailable and must contact the assigned PCP the next business day. That is related, but it does not specifically address an unknown prescriber.

similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy to act before dispensing when key information is missing or cannot be confirmed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources allow professional judgment or verification steps instead of a rigid automatic denial in the situations they describe [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual gives a direct rule for missing patient identifiers, while the policy only discusses emergency prescription handling and next-business-day PCP contact [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual requires a good-faith effort to verify an unknown prescriber, while the policy speaks more narrowly to emergency department prescriptions and does not state that same general duty [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
A supervising pharmacist must adhere to the following
responsibilities:
• The supervising pharmacist is responsible for the proper conduct of the pharmacy.
• The supervising pharmacist oversees the practice of other employed pharmacists and ensures that
those unlicensed persons do not engage in unlawful activity.
• The supervising pharmacist must have an active role in the conduct of the pharmacy and not a passive
role.
• The supervising pharmacist may not assign their responsibility to others.
• The supervising pharmacist must be aware of all laws and regulations and must instruct and direct
others to ensure compliance.
• The supervising pharmacist must work full-time (at least 30 hours per week). If the establishment is
operating less than 30 hours per week, the supervising pharmacist must work the majority of the hours.
• If the supervising pharmacist is to be absent from a pharmacy at any time, the supervising pharmacist
must ensure another qualified pharmacist is present in their absence.
• The supervising pharmacist cannot supervise more than one pharmacy establishment at a time.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Record Retention, Furnishing of Records, and Permitting Audit and Cooperation
with Governmental Agencies
As New York State (NYS) Medicaid-enrolled providers, in accordance with Title 18 of the New York Codes,
Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) §504.3(a) and §504.3(g), located at: https://regs.health.ny.gov/content/
section-5043-duties-provider, the pharmacy owner and the supervising pharmacist are responsible for:
• preparing and maintaining contemporaneous records demonstrating the right to receive payment
under the medical assistance program [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
• keeping for a period of six years from the date the care, services or supplies were furnished, all records
necessary to disclose the nature and extent of services furnished and all information regarding claims
for payment submitted by, or on behalf of, the provider [refer to 18 NYCRR §504.3(a)];
Please note: Providers continue to be subject to any additional record retention requirements
outlined in their prior or current contracts with Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) or Managed
Long Term Care (MLTC) Plans or Subcontractors thereof, including retention for 10 years or
more as delineated therein.

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist may not need to enter the missing address, age, or CIN if it is already readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the requester, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. When the prescriber is unknown, the pharmacist must make a good-faith effort to verify the prescription’s validity and the prescriber’s identity.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the Medicaid member’s address, age, or CIN is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter it in two limited situations. The information must already be readily available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist must know the person requesting the fill, or the pharmacist must otherwise be satisfied that the prescription is legitimate. This rule applies to those missing patient details and does not change the separate requirements for controlled substances.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update does not address missing patient demographics in this row. It instead says the pharmacy must use professional judgment when filling prescriptions from an emergency department visit if the assigned PCP is unavailable, and it must contact the assigned PCP the next business day. That is a narrower and less specific match than the provider manual guidance.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: If the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist, the pharmacist is responsible for making a good-faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. The manual states this as an affirmative pharmacist responsibility. It is a direct verification duty, not just a general caution.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update does not state a general rule for verifying an unknown prescriber. It instead requires professional judgment in a restricted-recipient emergency department scenario and follow-up with the assigned PCP the next business day. That makes it related, but broader and less specific than the provider manual.

similarities: Both sources place responsibility on the pharmacist or pharmacy when prescription information is incomplete or needs confirmation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources allow a judgment-based response rather than a rigid automatic denial in the situations they describe [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual gives a specific rule for missing address, age, or CIN, while the policy update does not address those patient demographics and instead discusses emergency department and restricted-recipient handling [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

The provider manual gives a direct good-faith verification duty for an unknown prescriber, while the policy update only gives a related but narrower follow-up workflow for emergency department prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible to replace lost, stolen, or mis-directed drugs at no additional cost to the
member or the Medicaid Program.
• Pharmacy providers who deliver medication without member or authorized individual consent will be
required to accept the return of the medication, credit Medicaid the claim, and destroy those drugs per
State Law.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs not picked up or delivered must be reversed within 60 days or sooner as
required by the mandatory part of New York State’s compliance programs under 18 NYCRR §521.

answers.headline_summary: When required patient information is missing, the pharmacist does not have to enter the member’s address, age, or CIN if that information is already readily available in the pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy requires the pharmacy to use professional judgment when filling prescriptions written during an emergency department visit if the assigned primary care provider is unavailable. test_id_41.query_content: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest, encourage, or otherwise require that any practitioner
administered, or dispensed drug be billed to the Program under the pharmacy benefit. Practitioner administered drugs
will continue to be billed as a NYS Medicaid medical benefit.
Questions regarding this policy may be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy unit at (518)486-3209 or
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement. The provider manual also says compounds may not be made for excluded indications such as drugs to treat weight loss.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the State Plan coverage list. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are not reimbursable under the policy update.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications. That includes drugs to treat weight loss.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications. That includes drugs to treat weight loss.

similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also say compounded products may not be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more operational because it ties the exclusion to the Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs list and to compound claim rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it states the exclusion as a coverage limitation under state plan coverage and gives examples of nonreimbursable drug uses [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement. The current provider manual is the newest source and confirms that compounds also may not be made for excluded weight loss indications.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid does not reimburse drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This is a direct coverage exclusion in the pharmacy manual. It applies to the drug use itself, not just to a specific product form.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYS Medicaid also lists drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain as not reimbursable. The policy update ties the exclusion to SSA §1927(d)(2). It is broader than the manual because it presents the exclusion as an example within state plan coverage limits.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The manual also says compounds may not be made to bypass Medicaid criteria. This means compounding cannot be used to work around the exclusion.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says compounds may not be made for excluded indications under SSA §1927(d)(2), including drugs to treat weight loss. It is the closest policy match to the compounding rule. It is slightly less detailed than the manual because it does not add the same operational language about bypassing criteria.

similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also say compounds cannot be used for excluded weight loss indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more operational because it also says compounds may not be made to bypass Medicaid criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it frames the exclusion as part of the list of drugs and drug uses that are not reimbursable under state plan coverage [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].
