policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

answers.headline_summary: Yes. NYS Medicaid excludes drugs used for weight loss from reimbursement, and the provider manual also says compounded products may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications such as weight loss. The current provider manual is the most up-to-date source here, and it confirms the exclusion applies to both standard drug coverage and compounded products.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Compounded products may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss. The manual also says compound ingredients are reimbursed only if they are on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable Drugs and are compendia-supported for the intended route and indication.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says the same exclusion for compounded products. It states that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2), including drugs to treat weight loss.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the NYS List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs. Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are listed as not reimbursable under Social Security Act section 1927(d)(2).
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says the same general coverage rule. It states that NYS Medicaid only reimburses drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and it specifically lists drugs used for weight loss as not reimbursable.

similarities: Both sources say drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

Both sources also apply the exclusion to compounded products that are made for weight-loss indications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines; New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual is more operational because it adds compound-claim requirements, including the reimbursable-drug list and compendia support for the intended route and indication [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader and less specific because it lists weight-loss drugs as an example of nonreimbursable drugs without the same compound-claim detail [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.text:
It is the responsibility of the covered entity
(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)

423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable

Not applicable

to Cash Customers Customers

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.text:
Medical Exception Request
If a prescriber has determined that a drug, not on the NY Medicaid List of Reimbursable Drugs is the only treatment
appropriate for a member, the prescriber may submit a letter of medical necessity and supporting documentation to
NYRx by fax at 518-473-5508 for a review of available coverage
4.0 Basis of Payment
Covered Outpatient Drugs (COD) are defined in federal statute.
Prescription Drugs
Pharmacy reimbursement for prescription drugs under the New York State Medicaid Program is established in law.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

The policy update is broader because it also covers NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care, and secondary claims [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NY Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs based on 340B claim-level identification and the 340B acquisition cost methodology. The current Pharmacy Manual says NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File and instead relies on 340B claim identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts; the pricing table shows 340B purchased drugs reimburse at 340B Acquisition Cost, subject to U&C if lower.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. It relies completely on 340B claim-level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. Those identifiers are required at claim submission, and they are the only way NY Medicaid will remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update says 340B drugs must be identified on the claim so rebates are not collected for those drugs. It also says missing or incorrect identifiers can make the covered entity responsible for reimbursing manufacturers. That is broader than the manual because it focuses on identification consequences rather than the Medicaid Exclusion File.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at 340B Acquisition Cost. Usual and customary pricing pays if it is lower. The pricing table lists this as the reimbursement method for eligible 340B purchased drug claims.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is less specific on payment methodology. It says 340B drugs on fee-for-service claims must use the UD modifier, report the NDC, and include actual acquisition cost in the charges field. That supports reimbursement processing, but it does not state the 340B Acquisition Cost pricing rule.

similarities: Both sources require claim-level identification for 340B drugs so Medicaid does not pay or rebate the same drug twice [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to claim submission details, not just the drug itself [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The manual says NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File and relies only on 340B claim identifiers, while the policy update focuses on the UD modifier, NDC reporting, and actual acquisition cost on fee-for-service claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The manual states the actual reimbursement rate for 340B purchased drugs, but the policy update does not state that pricing rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.

Not applicable

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 14

Physician-Administered Drugs (837P/837I)
An accurate National Drug Code (NDC) and “UD” modifier must be reported for any physician-administered
drug obtained at the 340B price that are billed on the Institutional claim form for either FFS or MMC. A
physician-administered drug will not be paid under Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) if the claim does not
include an accurate NDC. Please note: Any APGs fee schedule drug will still require a provider to code the
number of units and the acquisition cost for the claim line to be paid.
Non-Drug Items
As communicated in the December 2016 issue of the Medicaid Update cited and linked in the beginning of
this article, 340B claim level identifiers are required on all 340B purchased drug claims for Medicaid members.
Pharmacies should not submit claim level identifiers on non-340B eligible items, such as test strips. Diabetic
test strips are not covered outpatient drugs and are not part of the Medicaid Drug Rebate Program (MDRP),
and therefore are not eligible for a 340B discount. Only 340B purchased drugs should be tagged when claim
eligible with the 340B claim level identifiers.

(CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with the 340B stock
of the CE for 340B-eligible Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for these drugs. If rebates
are received by the NYS Department of Health (DOH) for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B claims billed to Medicaid FFS as secondary claims also
require identifiers, as well as 340B claims billed to MMC Plans.

NYRx by fax at 518-473-5508 for a review of available coverage

Covered Outpatient Drugs (COD) are defined in federal statute.

Pharmacy reimbursement for prescription drugs under the New York State Medicaid Program is established in law.

The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.

policy.text:
Ingredient cost submitted exceeds 340B ceiling price
2. Invalid codes submitted for 340B pharmacy drugs
The 340B ceiling price refers to the maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the covered entity for
the purchase of a 340B drug. A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid that is higher than the 340B ceiling price
will be denied. Claims that are denied must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. Alternatively, the
pharmacy may dispense a non-340B drug and remove the claim level identifiers and submit at the usual and
customary charge.
Claims for 340B drugs submitted to NYS Medicaid via the NCPDP D.0 format must also adhere to the
following:
• must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) members and Medicaid Managed
Care (MMC) enrollees; and
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members and MMC enrollees,
net any manufacturer discounts and/or other price reductions.

answers.headline_summary: NY Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs at the 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly identified as a 340B claim. The program uses claim-level 340B identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts, and incorrect or missing 340B identification can lead to denial or fraudulent billing findings.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File for 340B drug claims. It relies on claim-level 340B identifiers on all 340B drug claims to avoid duplicate discounts and remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B pharmacy drug claims must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service and Medicaid Managed Care. The policy is broader because it also states that the claim must be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drug claims reimburse at 340B Acquisition Cost. The pricing table also shows the usual dispensing fee structure, and claims may pay at usual and customary pricing if that is lower.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B claims submitted to NYS Medicaid must be identified as 340B and submitted at the 340B acquisition cost. If the ingredient cost exceeds the 340B ceiling price, the claim is denied and must be corrected or rebilled.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Applying claim-level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is fraudulent billing. The manual also requires specific NCPDP fields for 340B claims, including the submission clarification code and basis of cost determination.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: If a 340B claim is priced above the 340B ceiling price, it is denied and must be resubmitted with the correct ingredient cost. The policy is less specific about fraudulent billing, but it also allows the pharmacy to remove the 340B identifiers and bill a non-340B drug at usual and customary charge.

similarities: Both sources require 340B claims to be identified as 340B before Medicaid will process them correctly [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

Both sources tie payment to the 340B acquisition cost for properly identified 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].
differences: The provider manual focuses on NY Medicaid’s claim-level identifier approach and says the program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy update is broader because it also addresses denial and rebilling when the ingredient cost is above the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

The provider manual explicitly says that using claim-level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is fraudulent billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines]. The policy update instead emphasizes correction, denial, and the option to bill a non-340B drug at usual and customary charge [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2].

New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The information in this article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.

answers.headline_summary: NY Medicaid reimburses 340B drugs at 340B acquisition cost when the claim is properly identified as a 340B claim. The program also uses claim-level 340B identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts, and the usual dispensing fee methodology applies when the claim is eligible.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NY Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File for 340B claims. It relies on claim-level 340B identifiers on all 340B drug claims to avoid duplicate discounts and remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: All 340B-purchased drug claims billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care, or as secondary claims must include claim-level identifiers. That identification prevents rebates from being collected incorrectly. The policy is broader because it also names the billing pathways and secondary claims.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: 340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at 340B Acquisition Cost. A $10.18 professional dispensing fee applies when the claim is eligible for that fee and is not paid at usual and customary pricing.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: 340B-purchased drug claims submitted to NYS Medicaid must be submitted at actual acquisition cost. The policy states the payment basis, but it is less specific than the manual because it does not restate the dispensing fee detail.

similarities: Both sources require claim-level identification so 340B claims are not treated as rebate-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources tie 340B reimbursement to actual or acquisition cost rather than a standard drug pricing benchmark [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].
differences: The provider manual is more specific because it says NY Medicaid does not use the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File and instead relies completely on claim-level 340B identifiers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update is broader because it covers NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care, and secondary claims, while the manual focuses on the claim-level identification rule itself [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prescribers and Enrollment
Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be
enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an “Individual Billing” provider or as an “Ordering, Prescribing, Referring,
or Attending” (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products
and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx. There are two paths
of enrollment, as a biller or OPRA privder:
Physicians, Nurse Practitioners (NPs), Physician Assistants (PAs), Podiatrists, Dentists,
Optometrists, Audiologists, and Certified Nurse Midwives Providers who wish to:
1. Enroll and receive payment for covered NYS Medicaid services should apply as an “Individual Billing
Medicaid”. Information regarding how to enroll is available on the eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and
Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/; or
2. Enroll as an OPRA provider (non-billing) should alternatively enroll as an OPRA provider on the
eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/
info/ProviderEnrollment/.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not pay for home infusion through a bundled package. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the provider or pharmacy must bill other insurance first when applicable because Medicaid is the payer of last resort.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the Medicaid reimbursable drug list for pharmacy benefit coverage.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: NYRx covers covered products and services when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx. The update is broader than the manual because it explains the enrollment framework rather than the home infusion billing split. It supports coverage through the pharmacy benefit structure for Medicaid members.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit if they can be billed in NCPDP format, are delivered directly to the place of administration, are not included in a facility rate, and are dispensed by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. The manual treats this as one route for drugs that require administration by a practitioner.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Practitioner-administered drugs can be billed directly to Medicaid fee for service by a pharmacy when they are on the reimbursable drug list and are delivered directly to the site of administration. The update is less specific than the manual because it does not list every pharmacy-benefit criterion. It still matches the same pharmacy billing pathway for home infusion drugs.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The provider must determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurer covers the service being billed. If the service is covered, the provider must bill the other insurer before Medicaid because Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The billing responsibility sits with the provider.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The pharmacy is responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, including commercial plan, Medicare, facility, or Medicaid. The update is broader because it addresses general billing responsibility rather than the specific other-insurance sequence. It still confirms that the pharmacy must use the correct billing pathway.

similarities: Both sources say home infusion-related drugs can be covered through a pharmacy billing pathway rather than only through a medical claim [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9].

Both sources place responsibility on the billing provider or pharmacy to use the correct pathway and payer sequence [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The manual is specific that home infusion drugs and supplies are not paid as a bundled package, while the policy update is broader and focuses on NYRx enrollment and covered products [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9].

The manual gives detailed pharmacy-benefit criteria such as NCPDP billing, direct delivery, and no facility-rate inclusion, while the policy update only says practitioner-administered drugs may be billed by a pharmacy when delivered directly to the site of administration [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable

known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding

Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible for preparing and delivering the drug in accordance with administration
guidelines in the package insert, as well as the replacement of improperly stored, lost, or stolen drugs until
confirmed receipt by the authorized agent.
• The pharmacy must confirm an appropriate administration plan is in place for home deliveries.
• The pharmacy is required to obtain documentation of delivery by the receipt of a signature of a recipient or
an authorized agent at the site of administration.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs that were not deliverable must be reversed within 14 days.
• Once delivered and signed for, the site of administration is responsible for replacement of improperly
stored, handled, lost, or stolen practitioner-administered drugs.
“Brown bagging” is when drugs designated for self-administration or practitioner administration are dispensed directly
to a patient by the pharmacy. Brown bagging practitioner administered drugs causes concern regarding proper
storage or handling, which can affect the drug’s efficacy. Brown bagging is acceptable only when the drug is intended,
prescribed, or labeled for self-administration.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid does not pay a bundled home infusion rate for drugs, supplies, and services. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the pharmacy bills the drug when it is dispensed under the pharmacy pathway.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: New York State Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. The manual also points to the Medicaid reimbursable drug list for pharmacy benefit coverage.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy says practitioner administered drugs, including home infusion drugs, may be billed through both the Medical Benefit and the Pharmacy Benefit. It is broader than the manual because it describes the general billing pathways rather than the home infusion billing rule alone. It also notes that eligible drugs may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit if they meet pharmacy coverage criteria. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format and dispensed by a Medicaid enrolled pharmacy. The manual also requires delivery to the place of administration and other pharmacy benefit conditions.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: Eligible practitioner administered drugs may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy. The policy is less specific than the manual because it does not restate the pharmacy coverage criteria. It instead confirms the direct billing pathway for qualifying drugs.

similarities: Both sources say home infusion drugs are not paid through a bundled home infusion rate and instead use separate billing pathways [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14].

Both sources allow pharmacy billing for eligible home infusion or practitioner administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14].
differences: The provider manual gives the current operational criteria for pharmacy benefit coverage, including NCPDP billing and a Medicaid enrolled dispensing pharmacy, while the policy update mainly states that direct billing to NYRx is allowed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Paper claim submission and multiple drug procedure codes, including wastage, must be submitted for the
same date of service on separate claim forms. Additional information on paper claim submission can be found
in the Billing Instructions for Physician-Administered Drugs (J-codes) Submitted on Paper Claims article,
published in the December 2008 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2008/2008-12.htm#ins.
Practitioner Billing
Reimbursement for drugs, including vaccines and immune globulins, furnished by practitioners to their
patients, is based on the acquisition cost to the practitioner. For all items furnished in this fashion, practitioners
are expected to maintain auditable records of the actual itemized invoice cost of the drug. This includes the
numbers of doses of the drug represented on the invoice. NYS Medicaid FFS does not intend to pay more
than the actual acquisition cost of the drug dosage, as established by invoice, to the practitioner.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prescribers should use their professional judgement to determine the best method for members to obtain practitioner
administration drugs. It is the responsibility of the pharmacist to ensure white or brown bagged drugs are
appropriately dispensed.
This policy refers to any drug being dispensed by a pharmacy for practitioner-administration to a NYRx member,
including those billed as a secondary payment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: NYS Medicaid does not pay a bundled rate for home infusion. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, and the clearest billing responsibility in the retrieved evidence is that the pharmacy must bill timely and to the appropriate party.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: NYS Medicaid does not provide a bundled payment for drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit. This is the current home-infusion billing pathway in the provider manual.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update does not describe the home-infusion billing pathway directly. It instead states that practitioner-administered drugs given by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when the indication is FDA-approved or medically accepted and clinical criteria are met. That is broader and less specific than the provider manual.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit when they meet the pharmacy-benefit criteria in the manual. The drug must be billable in NCPDP format, delivered directly to the place of administration, not included in a facility rate, and dispensed by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. The manual also requires pharmacist counseling and record retention.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update gives a broader coverage standard for infused drugs. It says drugs administered by a practitioner are reimbursable when the FDA-approved or medically accepted indication requirement is met and the applicable clinical criteria are satisfied. It does not give the pharmacy-billing operational requirements from the manual.

similarities: Both sources recognize coverage for infused drugs when the service meets Medicaid coverage rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

Both sources tie payment to specific eligibility conditions rather than automatic coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
differences: The provider manual focuses on the home-infusion billing pathway, while the policy update focuses on practitioner-administered drug coverage criteria [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].

The provider manual gives pharmacy-benefit billing requirements such as NCPDP format and Medicaid-enrolled dispensing pharmacy, but the policy update does not [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8].
answers.caveats: The retrieved policy update is only a partial match for the home-infusion question because it addresses practitioner-administered drug coverage rather than home-infusion billing directly.

policy.text:
August 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require
Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs requiring administration by a practitioner
to be available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit. Such practitioner-
administered drugs are listed on the Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs
(https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx) and may be billed directly to the Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS)
program by a pharmacy. The intent of this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of
such drugs. Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest that all practitioner-administered drugs must be
dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding practitioner-administered drug billing is addressed in
the Physician Manual found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Physician/index.aspx.
Practitioner-administered drugs dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit must be delivered by the pharmacy directly to
the site of administration.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2022 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 10

Pharmacy
Pharmacy Dispensing Drugs that Require Practitioner
Administration
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program recognizes the need for drugs that must be administered by a
practitioner to be billable to NYS Medicaid members through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit.
Such practitioner administered drugs (PADs) are listed on the NYS Department of Health (DOH) List of
Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs document, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid Pharmacy List of Reimbursable
Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx, and may be billed directly to NYRx, formerly
known as the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy.
This guidance provides information for the proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs and is not
meant to suggest that all PADs must be dispensed as a Pharmacy Benefit. The policy regarding
pharmacy billing of drugs that require practitioner-administration can be found in the New York State Medicaid
Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, under the “Delivery” section.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Prescribers and Enrollment
Providers who prescribe drugs and supplies for NYS Medicaid members and/or MMC enrollees must be
enrolled in NYS Medicaid as either an “Individual Billing” provider or as an “Ordering, Prescribing, Referring,
or Attending” (OPRA) provider for NYS Medicaid to cover the products. NYRx will pay for covered products
and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx. There are two paths
of enrollment, as a biller or OPRA privder:
Physicians, Nurse Practitioners (NPs), Physician Assistants (PAs), Podiatrists, Dentists,
Optometrists, Audiologists, and Certified Nurse Midwives Providers who wish to:
1. Enroll and receive payment for covered NYS Medicaid services should apply as an “Individual Billing
Medicaid”. Information regarding how to enroll is available on the eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and
Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/; or
2. Enroll as an OPRA provider (non-billing) should alternatively enroll as an OPRA provider on the
eMedNY “Provider Enrollment and Maintenance” web page, located at: https://www.emedny.org/
info/ProviderEnrollment/.

provider_manual.text:
• The pharmacy is responsible for preparing and delivering the drug in accordance with administration
guidelines in the package insert, as well as the replacement of improperly stored, lost, or stolen drugs until
confirmed receipt by the authorized agent.
• The pharmacy must confirm an appropriate administration plan is in place for home deliveries.
• The pharmacy is required to obtain documentation of delivery by the receipt of a signature of a recipient or
an authorized agent at the site of administration.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs that were not deliverable must be reversed within 14 days.
• Once delivered and signed for, the site of administration is responsible for replacement of improperly
stored, handled, lost, or stolen practitioner-administered drugs.
“Brown bagging” is when drugs designated for self-administration or practitioner administration are dispensed directly
to a patient by the pharmacy. Brown bagging practitioner administered drugs causes concern regarding proper
storage or handling, which can affect the drug’s efficacy. Brown bagging is acceptable only when the drug is intended,
prescribed, or labeled for self-administration.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: Home infusion drugs may be covered under the pharmacy benefit if they can be billed in NCPDP format, delivered directly to the place of administration, are not included in a facility rate, and are dispensed by a Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy. These are the operational conditions for pharmacy-side coverage.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is less specific on home infusion billing criteria. It says the prescriber must be enrolled in NYS Medicaid as an Individual Billing or OPRA provider, and that NYRx pays for covered products when the prescriber is enrolled in NYRx.

aligned_pairs.provider_manual: The pharmacy is responsible for preparing and delivering the drug in accordance with administration guidelines. It must confirm an appropriate administration plan for home deliveries, obtain delivery signature documentation, and reverse claims for drugs that were not deliverable.
aligned_pairs.policy_update: The policy update is a closer but still broader match. It says practitioner-administered drugs may be billed directly to NYRx by a pharmacy, and it frames the guidance as instructions for proper dispensing and delivery.

similarities: Both sources reject a bundled home-infusion payment and place the drugs on a pharmacy or medical pathway instead [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9].

Both sources tie payment to pharmacy-side or practitioner-administration rules rather than a single bundled home-infusion rate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual gives the current home-infusion billing conditions in detail, while the policy update is broader and focuses on prescriber enrollment and direct billing to NYRx [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9].

The provider manual assigns the pharmacy specific delivery and documentation duties, while the policy update only says the pharmacy may bill directly and explains proper dispensing and delivery [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update December 2022 Volume 38 Number 14].

policy.text:
February 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 4

Reminder: Pharmacy Coverage Includes Over the Counter Naloxone
New York State (NYS) Medicaid covers naloxone over the counter (OTC) when prescribed by the practitioner
of a NYS Medicaid member or dispensed to a NYS Medicaid member in compliance with the State-issued
naloxone standing order. Information regarding the naloxone standing order can be found in the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) Non-Patient Specific Prescription for Naloxone with Pharmacy Dispensing
Protocol document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/diseases/aids/general/opioid_overdose_prevention
/docs/naloxone_standing_order_pharmacies.pdf.
To comply with NYS laws, regulations, and NYS Medicaid policy, a pharmacy must submit a claim and
dispense naloxone OTC pursuant to a fiscal order or via a standing order.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health
